name: Tool Identification Relay
description: Speed matching game where students match physical tools with names and proper uses. Instructor demonstrates each tool briefly, then students identify by sight. Emphasizes tool names, basic function, and why each tool is designed that way.
materials: Hand tools (hammers, screwdrivers, wrenches, pliers, chisels)
learning_objectives: Identify common hand tools by sight
Match tools to their intended uses
Remember tool names consistently
Explain what each tool does in simple terms
variations: Add power tools to more advanced version
Timed relay competition between teams
Include 'bad uses' — what NOT to do with each tool

name: Workshop Safety Scenario Cards
description: Scenario-based decision making where students respond to workshop hazards. Cards describe dangerous situations (loose clothing near drill press, tool left on edge of workbench, etc.). Students identify the hazard and explain the safe procedure. Emphasizes safety culture over rote memorization.
learning_objectives: Recognize common workshop hazards
Explain consequences of unsafe practices
Recommend correct safety procedures
Understand why safety rules exist (prevention, not rules for rules' sake)
variations: Add time pressure — quick response decisions
Have students create their own scenarios
Include 'near miss' stories from real tradespeople

name: Build a Birdhouse Project
description: Complete woodworking project from design through assembly and finishing. Students measure, cut wood pieces, assemble with nails or screws, sand, and finish. Emphasizes precision measurement (1/4 inch), reading a plan, executing steps in order, and producing a functional project. Real outcome: a birdhouse that birds might actually use.
learning_objectives: Measure lumber accurately to 1/4 inch
Mark and cut wood to dimensions
Drill pilot holes for fasteners
Assemble pieces with proper fastening
Sand and finish for appearance and durability
Follow a multi-step project plan independently
variations: Younger students: pre-cut pieces, use nails only
Older students: design custom house, calculate wood needed
Advanced: dovetail roof, hinged clean-out door

name: Dovetail Joint Practice
description: Advanced woodworking technique: cutting hand-dovetailed joints. Students mark layout, saw by hand, and chisel out waste to create interlocking tails and pins. Produces a joint that's beautiful and incredibly strong. Emphasizes precision, patience, problem-solving, and craftsmanship. This is real woodworking, not a shortcut.
materials: Hardwood scraps (maple or cherry preferred, at least 3/4 inch thick)
learning_objectives: Lay out dovetails accurately with precision
Saw precisely along marked lines
Chisel away waste without splitting
Fit and adjust joint for tight assembly
Understand why this joint is strong
Develop fine motor control and spatial reasoning
variations: Start with 1/2 pins for easier first attempt
Progress to mixed tails and pins
Use jigs (advanced method) vs. pure hand layout
Combine two joints into a functional box

name: Simple Circuit Breadboard Lab
description: Build working circuits on breadboards with batteries, LEDs, resistors, and switches. Students understand voltage, current, series/parallel, and use multimeters to measure. Safe, no danger of shock. Real outcome: light comes on when you press the switch.
learning_objectives: Build series and parallel circuits correctly
Understand why resistors are needed (protect LEDs)
Use a multimeter to measure voltage and current
Troubleshoot a circuit that doesn't work
Explain what voltage, current, and resistance mean
variations: Younger: simple series circuits with one LED
Older: design a control circuit with multiple switches
Advanced: PWM dimming control

name: Wire a Light Switch (Model)
description: Safe 12V model of home electrical wiring. Students wire a switch to control a lamp, following proper wiring practices: correct wire gauges, secure connections, proper switch wiring (line in, load out), and safety. Uses low voltage so no shock hazard, but follows real residential standards. Students understand how the light switch in their bedroom actually works.
materials: Drawings of circuit and wiring diagram
learning_objectives: Understand switch operation (break and make contact)
Follow electrical diagrams and schematics
Strip and secure wire connections properly
Verify correct wiring with a multimeter
Explain why a switch must be in the 'hot' line
Troubleshoot a wiring error
variations: Three-way switches (two switches control one light)
Dimmer switch (demonstrates variable resistance)
Multiple light circuits with circuit breaker protection

name: PVC Pipe Plumbing Assembly
description: Assemble plumbing using PVC pipe, fittings (elbows, tees, unions), and PVC cement. Students build a small system, test it with water, and identify any leaks. Emphasizes proper fitting selection, primer, cement application, and pressure testing. Real outcome: a system that holds water without leaks.
materials: Assorted fittings: elbows, tees, unions, adapters
learning_objectives: Identify pipe and fitting types
Cut pipe squarely
Apply primer and cement correctly
Assemble fittings in proper orientation
Test system with water
Identify and fix leaks
Understand pressure and flow
variations: Young: pre-assembled fittings, dry fitting practice
Older: design a water distribution system
Advanced: add ball valves and drains

name: Water Pressure Experiment
description: Hands-on demonstration of water pressure principles using containers, tubes, and valves. Students observe that pressure depends on height and depth, understand why dams are thicker at the bottom, understand why tall buildings need pressure tanks, and discover why your shower pressure drops when someone flushes. Turns abstract physics into tangible experience.
materials: Small objects to demonstrate buoyancy and pressure
learning_objectives: Understand that water pressure increases with depth
Explain why pipes need support (weight of water)
Understand why buildings need pressure regulators
Predict what happens when you add/remove water from the system
Explain flow rate and pressure relationship
variations: Younger: simple height demonstrations, play with water flow
Older: calculate pressure at various depths
Advanced: explore siphoning and trap principles

name: Sheet Metal Box Fabrication
description: Cut, bend, and join sheet metal to create a functional box. Students use metal shears to cut flat stock, a bending brake to create folds, and either rivets or self-tapping screws to assemble. Emphasizes planning before cutting, precision measurement, and understanding how sheet metal behaves. Real outcome: a metal box suitable for holding tools or parts.
materials: Bending brake or fabrication table with clamping
Pop rivet tool and rivets OR #6 self-tapping screws and screwdriver
learning_objectives: Lay out a box design and calculate material needed
Measure and mark metal for cutting
Cut sheet metal safely with proper tools
Bend metal using a brake without over-bending
Assemble metal with rivets or screws properly
Understand grain direction and work hardening in metals
variations: Younger: simpler 4-side box, pre-bent sides provided
Older: custom design, integrated hinges for lid
Advanced: powder coat or paint the finished box

name: Tack Welding Practice (Simulation)
description: Practice basic welding technique in a safe environment (simulation or supervised low-amperage welding). Students learn proper positioning, correct arc length, travel speed, and bead formation. Emphasis on muscle memory and developing the feel for good technique. Real equipment under expert supervision or high-quality simulator software. Produces practice beads and tack welds.
materials: Welding workstation OR welding simulator software
Practice coupons (steel flat stock, 1/8 inch thick)
Wire feeder and shielding gas (if real welding) OR simulator system
learning_objectives: Don proper PPE without assistance
Strike an arc safely
Maintain proper arc length and travel speed
Produce a straight, clean bead with good fusion
Understand what causes common defects (porosity, undercut, cold joints)
Inspect welds for quality
Respect the dangers of welding (heat, UV, fumes)
variations: Start with simulator for technique development
Progress to real welding under supervision
Different joint types: flat, vertical, overhead
Weld actual parts together (small metal box, frame)
